title: Synergistic doping of the grain interior and grain boundary alters deformation mechanisms and enables extreme strength in nanocrystalline Ni-Cr-Y alloys
authors: Yi Liu, Jason R. Trelewicz, Timothy J. Rupert
abstract: Solid solution addition and grain boundary segregation have been independently shown to enhance the strength of nanocrystalline alloys. In the present study, the synergy between these two effects is investigated in nanocrystalline Ni-Cr-Y sputtered films through systematic variation of alloying element contents with grain size kept constant. Cr is introduced into a solid solution and serves to strengthen the lattice, while Y segregates to the grain boundaries to stabilize these features. Nanoindentation is used to probe hardness, with unexpected trends and very high values observed. Cr additions led to nanocrystalline solid solution strengthening, yet saturation was observed at higher concentrations due to the emergence of grain boundary dominated processes, as evidenced by pile-up morphologies containing slip steps and grain rotation. Y segregated to the grain boundaries, enhancing boundary-mediated strengthening by pinning the dislocations and suppressing dislocation emission, grain boundary sliding, and grain rotation processes. With increasing Y concentration, the nanocrystalline solid solution strengthening effect induced by Cr addition becomes weaker. This phenomenon can be attributed to a reduced dislocation bowing distance caused by dopant pinning. Most notably, the strongest ternary Ni-Cr-Y alloy exhibited a hardness of 11.0 GPa, among the highest hardness values reported for single-phase Ni-based alloys. These findings highlight how tuning grain and grain boundary chemistry offers a viable strategy to control dislocation mechanics and improve the strength of nanocrystalline metals.
summary: This paper demonstrates a strategy to achieve extreme hardness in nanocrystalline Ni-Cr-Y alloys by simultaneously doping the grain interior and grain boundaries. By using Yttrium to pin grain boundaries, the researchers suppressed grain boundary sliding, allowing Chromium-induced solid solution strengthening to reach much higher levels. This approach resulted in a hardness of 11.0 GPa, one of the highest reported for single-phase Ni-based alloys.
main_problem: Investigating how the synergistic doping of grain interiors (via Cr) and grain boundaries (via Y) can be used to control deformation mechanisms and achieve extreme hardness in nanocrystalline Ni-based alloys.
main_result: The ternary Ni-Cr-Y alloy achieved a record hardness of 11.0 GPa by using Y to suppress grain boundary sliding and rotation, thereby extending the range of Cr-induced solid solution strengthening.
method: The study uses magnetron co-sputtering to fabricate thin films, nanoindentation and AFM to probe mechanical properties and surface morphology, and TEM/STEM with EDS to analyze grain structure and chemical segregation.
model_or_system: Nanocrystalline Ni-Cr-Y thin films with a single-phase FCC structure and grain sizes of approximately 20-30 nm, where Cr acts as a lattice solute and Y acts as a grain boundary segregant.
key_observables: Hardness (GPa), lattice parameter, grain size, dislocation-mediated plasticity, grain boundary sliding, and grain rotation.
important_parameters: Cr and Y concentrations (at.%), grain size (20-30 nm), and effective dislocation obstacle spacing (L_eff).
main_assumptions: Cr is assumed to be a lattice-soluble solute, Y is assumed to be a grain boundary segregant, and the substrate influence on hardness is assumed to be negligible.
figures_summary: Fig 1: SEM and EDS maps of grain morphology and chemistry; Fig 2: XRD patterns and lattice parameter vs. Cr content; Fig 3: TEM grain size distribution; Fig 4: STEM/EDS analysis of Y segregation; Fig 5: Hardness vs. Cr concentration; Fig 6: Hardness plateau vs. Y concentration; Fig 7: Comparison with NC-SSS models; Fig 9: AFM topography of indentation pile-up; Fig 10: TEM of deformed grain structures.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of nanocrystalline strength, describes the fabrication and characterization of Ni-Cr-Y films, analyzes the individual and synergistic effects of Cr and Y on hardness and deformation mechanisms, compares results to theoretical models, and concludes with the implications for alloy design.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
